title: Truncated-Binary Encoding: Spectral Degree Reduction of Combinatorial Optimization Problems for Quantum Hardware
abstract: Exact-binary encoding compiles a discrete cost function network (CFN) into a higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) problem whose maximum monomial degree grows with the cardinalities of the underlying CFN variables. Given that quantum optimization hardware generally favours quadratic unconstrained binary optimization or low-degree HUBO Hamiltonians, high-cardinality CFNs therefore incur substantial overhead in the form of circuit depth, or ancilla qubits when degree-reduction techniques are employed. To ameliorate these issues, we propose \textit{truncated-binary encoding} (TBE): a modification of exact-binary encoding in which Ising-basis monomials exceeding a chosen cutoff $k_\text{max}$ are dropped from the encoded cost. We establish a tight $L^\infty$ bound on the truncation error in terms of the omitted couplings, derive sufficient conditions on the energy gap and on the single bit-flip basin barrier under which TBE preserves the global minimum and its local-minimum structure, and characterize a noise floor condition on the spectral profile under which the truncation residual acts as a perturbative correction to the underlying landscape. We then express the encoded coefficients directly as Walsh transforms of the underlying CFN cost tables, and prove a bound under which smoothness of each cost table implies rapid decay of its high-degree Walsh mass. Together these results yield a principled \textit{a priori} criterion for selecting $k_\text{max}$ and for judging whether a given CFN admits a small-$k_\text{max}$ TBE.
summary: This paper proposes a new method called Truncated-Binary Encoding to reduce the complexity of optimization problems mapped to quantum hardware. By strategically dropping high-degree interactions in the Ising basis, it minimizes hardware overhead while providing mathematical guarantees that the essential features of the optimization landscape remain intact.
main_problem: High-order interactions in the encoding of discrete cost functions into Ising models create high-degree monomials that are incompatible with the limited native locality of current quantum annealing and gate-based hardware.
main_result: The paper introduces Truncated-Binary Encoding (TBE), proving that discarding high-degree Ising monomials preserves the global minimum and local minimum structure under specific energy gap and basin barrier conditions.
method: The author uses Walsh-Hadamard spectral analysis to treat the encoding as a low-pass filter and employs the Lindeberg-Feller Central Limit Theorem to characterize the truncation error as a Gaussian noise floor.
model_or_system: The model consists of Cost Function Networks (CFNs) mapped to Ising-basis Higher-Order Unconstrained Binary Optimization (HUBO) problems, specifically targeting quantum annealing and gate-based architectures like QAOA.
key_observables: Truncation error (L-infinity norm), spectral power (P_k), energy gap (Delta E*), and the single bit-flip basin barrier (delta f).
important_parameters: The truncation cutoff degree (k_max), the maximum monomial degree (k_full), and the smoothness of cost tables (Lipschitz constants).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes an Ising-basis formulation and relies on a random-coupling ensemble where omitted couplings are treated as independent zero-mean random variables.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of high-order interaction overhead, introduces the TBE method, establishes error bounds and preservation conditions for minima, performs a spectral analysis using Walsh transforms, and concludes with a diagnostic workflow for selecting the truncation cutoff.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
